name: AcopioVzla
db_description:
Acopio nació como respuesta a un problema concreto: en situaciones de emergencia, la ayuda humanitaria se dispersa porque no existe información centralizada y actualizada sobre qué necesita cada centro de acopio. La comunicación ocurre por WhatsApp, el esfuerzo solidario se concentra en los centros más conocidos mientras otros quedan desabastecidos, y los voluntarios no saben a dónde ir ni qué llevar.
Acopio resuelve esto con una plataforma web simple: cada centro registra su perfil y publica actualizaciones en tiempo real sobre sus necesidades. La ciudadanía accede a un feed público donde puede ver, filtrar y decidir cómo ayudar de forma concreta e informada.
El proyecto es gratuito, sin publicidad y de acceso abierto. Fue construido con React, Vite, Supabase y PostGIS, con foco en ser liviano, rápido de adoptar y fácil de operar incluso desde un celular básico. Entre más centros participan, más útil se vuelve para todos — es infraestructura solidaria que escala con la comunidad.

analysis.summary: Acopio VZLA (also branded "Saman") is a React/TypeScript SPA backed by Supabase that maps humanitarian aid collection centers (centros de acopio) in Venezuela post-earthquake. Users can browse centers publicly, see what supplies are needed, follow real-time updates per center, leave comments, and post shelter listings (anuncios de hospedaje). The project was built in roughly 2 days by a single developer, is deployed on Vercel, and shows unusually strong engineering discipline for a hackathon: 10 versioned DB migrations, 98.99% line test coverage via Vitest + MSW, well-organized feature-based folder structure, and proper Supabase RLS on all tables. The main gap is no CI/CD and no server-side rate limiting or logging.
analysis.project_type: Frontend SPA (React + Vite) with Supabase BaaS

database: PostGIS (initial migration; coords removed in migration 00002)

architecture.pattern: Feature-based SPA with BaaS (Supabase). No custom server — all business logic lives in React hooks and Supabase RLS/DB triggers.
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Supabase JS client (supabase-js v2) with direct table queries and RPC calls. No ORM. Typed via generated database.generated.ts + hand-written db.ts types.
architecture.api_design: No custom REST/GraphQL API. The frontend calls Supabase REST endpoints directly. PostgreSQL triggers handle server-side side effects (e.g., notification on comment insert via notificar_comentario() trigger). 10 versioned SQL migrations managed via Supabase CLI.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clean: pages/ own layout, features/ own queries+mutations+realtime hooks (TanStack Query), components/ are presentational. Auth state via useSession() singleton hook.
architecture.modularity: High. Each feature (centros, posts, notificaciones, anuncios, auth) has isolated queries.ts / mutations.ts / realtime.ts. Test mocks live in src/test/mocks/ and are shared, not duplicated per feature.
notable_patterns: MSW (Mock Service Worker) for all Supabase calls in tests — no live DB in CI
TanStack Query with staleTime 30s / gcTime 5min for center listings
Supabase Realtime channel per centro — subscription cleaned up on unmount
DB-level RLS enforces ownership (coordinador_id = auth.uid()); frontend cannot bypass it
Client-side image compression via canvas.toBlob before Storage upload
PostgreSQL trigger (notificar_comentario) creates notifications server-side on comment insert
Nominatim geocoding with 1 req/s throttle for address-to-coord lookup

code_organization.directory_structure: src/ split into features/, components/, pages/, lib/, types/, test/. DB schema in supabase/migrations/ with 10 numbered migrations. Docs in docs/ (PRD.md + ARCHITECTURE.md). Very clean for a 2-day hackathon.
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent Spanish naming aligned with domain (centros_acopio, notificacion, anuncio) and English for code constructs. File names are descriptive and predictable.
code_organization.documentation_quality: Above average: README with full setup guide, troubleshooting, and commands table; separate PRD.md and ARCHITECTURE.md; SQL migrations with inline comments.
code_organization.reasoning: The codebase is unusually well-organized for a hackathon entry. Feature isolation is genuine, not cosmetic. The only minor gap is that some test files reside both inside src/features/ and src/components/ rather than a unified test/ directory.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Only two VITE_* env vars needed (Supabase URL + anon key). .env.example committed with placeholders. No secrets in code.
production_readiness.security_notes: RLS enforced on all tables including anuncio, notificacion, post_comentario. Storage policies scope uploads to auth.uid() folder prefix. coordinador_id is always set from auth.uid() server-side — cannot be spoofed by client. No custom backend means no SSRF/injection surface beyond Supabase defaults.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Supabase free tier scales to ~50k monthly active users. Real-time is scoped per-center channel (not a global broadcast). No pagination implemented on the feed — full table scan on posts per center on load. Nominatim geocoding is throttled client-side but could be a bottleneck under load.
production_readiness.reasoning: Solid security posture via Supabase RLS and proper ownership checks. No CI/CD pipeline, no server-side logging or error monitoring (no Sentry etc.), and no Dockerfile. The app is deployed on Vercel already, which counts as deploy config, but it is implicit (no vercel.json committed).

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real — contains substantial domain logic: 10 versioned DB migrations, 7700+ LOC, fully typed Supabase queries, MSW test mocks with fixtures, and a live deployed instance at acopiovzla.vercel.app.
maturity.reasoning: 98.99% line coverage with Vitest + React Testing Library + MSW is genuinely impressive for a 2-day hackathon. However there is no CI/CD (no .yml files found), a single contributor, and only 21 commits — it is a solid MVP, not a mature product.

viability.reasoning: Solves a clear, immediate problem (locating and tracking aid collection centers post-earthquake) with a working deployed instance and a full feature set: center registry, real-time updates, comments, notifications, and shelter listings. The Supabase BaaS backend means near-zero ops overhead. Main deployment risk is Supabase free-tier limits under surge traffic and no incident monitoring.

key_features: Public browsable map/grid of aid collection centers (centros de acopio) — no login required
Real-time post feed per center via Supabase Realtime (WebSocket, 2 events/sec throttle)
Supplies-needed tags (necesidades[]) on posts with predefined + custom chip selector
Shelter listings (anuncios de hospedaje) with capacity, pets, accessibility fields
DB trigger auto-notifies center coordinator on new comment
Client-side image compression (canvas JPEG 0.8) before Supabase Storage upload
Address geocoding via Nominatim (OpenStreetMap) with 1 req/s throttle
Supabase Auth with email confirmation flow, resend support
Community feed for posts not tied to a specific center
Mobile-first responsive layout with desktop sidebar
analysis.merge_potential: High — clean feature boundaries, strong test suite with MSW mocks, and a BaaS backend mean another team could fork or extend individual features (e.g., add mapping-geo visualisation, people-finder module) with low integration risk. The SQL migration system makes schema changes traceable.
red_flags: Coordinates (lat/lng) and PostGIS centros_cercanos RPC were removed in migration 00002 — the proximity-search capability advertised in the README no longer exists; centers are now sorted alphabetically by city only
No CI/CD pipeline — tests are not run automatically on push; coverage numbers come from a committed coverage-summary.json, not a gated check
No pagination on post/center feeds — a center with thousands of posts will fetch all of them in a single query
No server-side rate limiting beyond Nominatim throttle — registration and post creation are only rate-limited by Supabase free-tier defaults
Single contributor, 21 commits across 2 days — bus factor of 1 with very short history
